slides.title:
Who Will Control the
AI Infrastructure
of Higher Education?
slides.subtitle: From useful tools to structural dependence, and how universities can still shape their future
slides.author: Dr Stuart Grey
slides.badge: Future Facing Learning and AI in Higher Education 2026

notes.transition: Open with the question, not with housekeeping.
points: Frame the keynote as a shift from 'how should we use AI?' to 'who controls the AI we use?'
You will hear talks about pedagogy, assessment, and practice.
This talk is neither anti-AI nor anti-industry; it is about governance.

voiceover.transcript: Hi, and good afternoon everyone. My name is Dr. Stuart Gray, and today I'll be talking to you about AI infrastructure, and in particular AI infrastructure in education. And we've seen lots of talks these next few days on pedagogy and assessment and practice. But what I would like to discuss today is a shift from how should we use AI to who controls the AI we use. And one of the things I'd like to point out is this talk is neither anti-AI nor anti-industry. It's about governance.

notes.transition: Move from the big question to why I am the person making this argument.
points: So, what's my angle?

voiceover.transcript: So why listen to me? So there's two real parts about why I'm in a position to hopefully make this argument in a relatively persuasive manner.

notes.transition: Take the first half of that perspective: technical grounding.
points: Use this to show I have a machine learning / AI research background, not just a policy interest.
The point is that I understand AI systems as systems with architecture, coordination, and failure modes.

voiceover.transcript: The first is my technical background. I did a PhD in Distributed AI Systems. Since then, I've moved to use those tools of my career into more of my teaching and learning role. I also still teach part-time at University of Glasgow. I have the embedded academic experience and also the technical know-how. That domain knowledge and the technical know-how about how these models will work. I can create these models, test them, and understand hopefully a little bit about where they've been and a tiny bit about where they might go.

slides.title: Free Text Analysis with Student Voice AI

notes.transition: Then pivot from technical grounding to applied institutional grounding.
points: Explain that most of my day-to-day work is free-text analysis with UK education institutions through Student Voice AI.
This means I see what AI and data practices look like inside real institutional workflows, not only in theory.
Say explicitly that the talk comes from looking at both the technology and the institutional consequences.

voiceover.transcript: And I've also founded and run a spin-out called Student Voice AI, where we analyze free text student comments for universities across the UK, including eight Russell Group universities. So again, this gives me a real insight into the university ecosystem, the current problems and issues around use of AI. So between those two, my academic background, the technical background, hopefully I can have something to say about where we are and where we're going.

notes.transition: Now give the room the first of three lenses for reading the rest of the talk.
points: Transparency asks: what can institutions actually see, inspect, explain, and contest?
Use it to frame provenance, auditability, model dependencies, data use, and institutional legibility.

voiceover.transcript: Now, throughout today's talk, I'd like you to keep three things in mind, if possible. We've got three kind of angles to what I'll be talking about in terms of AI infrastructure. And the first is transparency, as in, do we have any visibility into this infrastructure? As things become more and more important, the transparency becomes more and more important. And currently, we're relying on these models on trust. And I'll make some arguments about why perhaps that trust is misplaced, especially with some of these companies involved. So do we have access? Are we able to audit, explain what is coming into and out of these models? Are we able to see what training data was used to create them? Are we able to see what decisions were made around how they were post-trained, et cetera? So lots of things on transparency. So that's the first thing to bear in mind.

notes.transition: Add the second lens by shifting from visibility to shape.
points: Distortion asks what gets bent when systems reward what is easy to capture, measure, or automate.
This will later connect to assessment design, pedagogy, workflow simplification, and institutional incentives.

voiceover.transcript: The next is about distortion. And there's two aspects to this. The first is the thing we probably have heard a lot about is that these models don't tell the truth or any sort of objective truth, right? They are there, they're designed to interact with people, they're designed to continue to keep those interactions going and as such, things get distorted, right? They can be overly sycophantic, right? They can just hide certain things, not discuss certain things, right? And that could be a definite benefit. You don't want them explaining how to make a bomb or whatever, but you do need to understand how they are distorting things, what their limitations are, what their designs are making them do, and if that's the right decision for our use or not. The other type of distortion is as we engage with these tools and they become more infrastructure, it will distort our ways of working as well. And I think that's maybe slightly less discussed and I think that's something I would like to highlight throughout this talk.

notes.transition: Complete the triad with the political-economy lens.
points: Extraction asks who captures value when educational activity becomes data, workflow, or dependency.
Later this will connect to data use, vendor leverage, upstream value capture, and platform power.

voiceover.transcript: And the last of the three lenses is about extraction. So really it's about incentives. Who is making money off this? Who is paying lots of money for these tools? Who has assets that are worthwhile? Who is taking control of those assets? Who is deciding what is used and what's not used? And I think we can maybe get some hints there, the way I'm talking even, about why I think about that. But again, another third lens to talk about it. So we have transparency, distortion, and extraction.

slides.text: The argument in higher education is no longer about whether AI tools are impressive. It is about who decides what we can do with AI.
slides.highlight: It is about who decides what we can do with AI

notes.transition: Now make the frame explicit and turn the lenses into the keynote thesis.
points: Say that the argument is no longer mainly about whether the tools are impressive.
Say the deeper question is who sets the terms on which we use AI as AI becomes normal infrastructure.
Use this as the bridge from the opening frame into the body of the talk.

voiceover.transcript: And an important part of what I'd like to do is to skip beyond the arguments about whether these tools are impressive or useful in any given context, because I think we can all hopefully list areas where they are very useful, right? Unless you're really diehard anti-AI, in which case, okay, it's a very different discussion we're having there. But I think they can be seen to be useful for certain tasks. But that's not what I'm talking about, right? I want to talk about who sets the terms on which we use this AI. Who decides how we can use it? Who decides what it's useful for, right? And that maps across all of our three angles.

slides.title: From tool to infrastructure
slides.subtitle: How AI moved from optional assistance to background infrastructure

notes.transition: Having set up the lenses, move into the first descriptive claim.
points: This section explains how AI moved from visible tool to background infrastructure.
Use the subtitle to tell the audience we are now moving from framing to diagnosis.
The job here is to make 'infrastructure' feel concrete.

voiceover.transcript: So, in this first section, I'd like to talk about what happens when something goes from a useful tool we are using, a very specific use case, to becoming part of infrastructure. And we can look back at some examples of this and hopefully think about ourselves about we can see this happening in real time, but what are the issues and what should we be aware of in this case?

slides.heading: AI is moving from visible tools to invisible systems
slides.body: The important shift is not simply more campus use. It is that AI is being stitched into teaching, assessment, student support, research workflows and institutional decision-making faster than governance is catching up. When that happens, AI stops looking like a product choice and starts behaving like infrastructure.

notes.transition: Start with the broad institutional picture before going to evidence.
points: AI is no longer just a tool someone opens; it is becoming part of how teaching, assessment, support, research, and governance are carried out.
Stress both the shift from optional use to embedded workflow and the lag in institutional coordination.
Say that once many activities assume the system is there, AI stops behaving like a product choice and starts behaving like infrastructure.
Teaching and assessment: Generative tools now sit inside drafting, feedback, marking support and redesign of assessment.
Student support and admin: Copilots, triage systems and analytics are creeping into advising, helpdesks and routine services.
Research and governance: Search, synthesis, coding, grant development and management reporting increasingly run through AI-mediated layers.

voiceover.transcript: So, when we first started using these tools, I think ChatGPT was the quintessential initial moment. Okay, it's a chat box. It's a tool you go to. You can copy and paste things in, but it's a very separate entity. And this had real use, okay, across teaching and assessment, across student support and admin, research and governance, for right or wrong, right, especially the early versions, right? Major issues, but there were obvious use cases where you could copy something from some work into ChatGPT, get it to reword it, put it back, ask it for ideas, right, but it's a very separate thing. But what we see now are this movement from that specific use, that sort of encapsulated siloed use, to it kind of permitting in the background. It just exists now. And you draft something and it just pops up, okay, here's a new draft. There's a little button in the tools you're using. There are options to use it in different LTIs, in VLEs, in all the office suites we use, right? And it's permeating the whole thing and it becomes part of the substrate. That's a really important movement because this is a time, once that happens, it's just baked in and we don't have any choice anymore. We can't decide to go between ChatGPT and Claude anymore. You have to use whatever's built in. And we're finding this already with some of the co-pilot use institutions. So as things become more deliberate and made out of choice, they become more automatic and defined by whoever makes that decision once, and then that's the only way it can happen. And that is really dangerous.

slides.heading: Student adoption is already mainstream
slides.body: The governance problem is immediate because student practice has already normalised faster than institutional structures have adapted.

stats.label: of UK full-time undergraduates use AI in at least one way
use generative AI to help with assessed work
say assessment has changed significantly because of AI

notes.transition: Back the previous claim with numbers so the room feels the urgency.
points: Use the HEPI figures to show this is already mainstream student practice.
Link the assessment figure directly to the conference context: the room already knows assessment is changing.
The point is not surprise at student use; the point is that institutional governance can no longer lag behind it.

voiceover.transcript: Now, to put some numbers on this adoption in that the use of the tool has skyrocketed. Vast majority of UK undergraduates use AI in at least some way. They've used it with assessed work, and most people agree that assessment has changed significantly because of AI. Again, mostly for the worst, but sometimes for the better. There are other options there. But this tidal wave is happening, and that was just with the standalone tools. This is before we bake it into everything we're doing, which is slowly, and I think perhaps inevitably happening. You have to decide, okay, if this is actually happening, what are we going to do about it? Where do we say no? Where do we say, okay, we'll do something different? Or where do you say, okay, no, that's actually fine? Where's the governance here?

slides.heading: Universities need a viable system for AI governance, not just a policy
slides.body: Many institutions already have local experimentation. What they often lack is coordination, assurance, foresight and mission-level steering across the whole system, especially when teaching continuity depends on external platforms.

notes.transition: Before leaving the opening diagnosis, turn it into the first institutional implication.
points: If governance is lagging behind use, the answer cannot be a thin guidance document.
The institution needs coordination, assurance, foresight and mission-level steering across the whole system.
Use this as the first practical move so the talk does not leave action until the end.
Coordinate the edge: Join up local experimentation so teaching, support, procurement and data decisions are not scattered.
Build assurance: Test tools, document risk and create routes for review and contestation before high-stakes use.
Steer by mission: Make senior governance articulate what kind of university the AI stack is supposed to serve.

voiceover.transcript: And this is where we get to the difference between governance and policy. Now, all institutions, rightly, I'm not picking on anyone here, are producing AI policies, right? Lots of hand-wringing and lots of hard work by everyone. But we don't need policies because what we need is that step beyond policy, which is governance, which is policy with teeth, right? With action, with autonomy built in, right? Which means coordination between different users. We need assurance, testing. And I'll be building on this a lot as we go on through this talk. How are we sure this is doing what we do? Where are the tests? I'm a systems engineer by training. How are we testing this? How are we verifying it? How are we validating it? And those two are very specific things. And perhaps most importantly, how are we making sure that it's all going towards the mission of our higher education institutions? Now, they will be different by institution, but generally they are for the societal good, for education and research, right? Quite noble, I think. And we have to make sure that that is baked into all the decisions. And that's a governance issue, not a policy issue, right? The policy can say what it likes or what you should and shouldn't do. But governance is making sure what actually happens on the ground and pushing in those directions, okay?

slides.title: The bubble
slides.subtitle: Why the boom matters even if the hype does not last

notes.transition: Having sketched the first implication, zoom back out to the upstream build-out behind it.
points: This section moves from campus use to the political economy of the wider stack.
Signal that the next slides are not dismissing AI as hype; they are asking what durable capacity the boom is creating, and for whom.
This is the bridge from institutional dependence to the infrastructure being built upstream.

voiceover.transcript: So what's the backdrop to this scenario? So we can take a step back. And I think we can all agree, we're in some sort of AI bubble, right? Now, if you're being unkind, you call it a bubble. If you're being kind, you call it a boom, right? Both the same thing. A absolutely tremendous amount of money is being spent rolling out these systems and to train them and to inference, okay, so give the answers to people. And I think the scale of it is really important. And I think we've got to understand that scale, where we sit within that and where that relationship is and where we can go from there.

slides.heading: Tech companies are building capacity universities may later inherit
slides.body: The current AI boom is not just hype. It is also an enormous build-out of compute, models, tooling and organisational knowledge that may outlast the frenzy. If universities are strategic, some of that capacity can become cheaper access, better tools and more credible public-interest alternatives.

notes.transition: Open the section by separating hype from infrastructure.
points: The key line is that speculative capital can still build real capacity.
Point to compute, models, tooling and organisational learning as durable inheritances of the current boom.
Say that the question is not whether infrastructure is being built; it is who will control what is left behind.
Compute and tooling: Data centres, GPU fleets, orchestration frameworks and evaluation tooling are being hardened quickly.
Open models and hardware: Maturing open models and post-boom hardware markets can make sector alternatives more plausible over time.
Institutional learning: Universities are also learning fast about policy, pedagogy and workflow design while the ecosystem is moving.

voiceover.transcript: Now, this AI bubble is huge. Billions of dollars are being wasted because not everyone can win. Not everyone can possibly make enough money back to pay off the investors for these things. So a lot of money will be lost. But I honestly think it's a good thing, right? Bubbles can have real benefits. Now, when everyone talks about, okay, what's an example of a financial bubble, people go to tulips, right? In the 1600s. But that's actually a very remarkably different type of bubble than most, right? That is a purely financial bubble. That could be equated maybe with cryptocurrency, right? They're all on a par. The vast majority of bubbles we've had before have actually pushed forward significantly the technology and the ability to do things. So telecommunications, okay, bubbles building out loads of hardware for telephones, for internet, right? Railroads, okay? Tremendous amounts of investment needed and lots of companies have bust. But what they left behind was railways. What they left behind was phone networks. So what's going to be left behind? There'll be no love lost if Anthropic and OpenAI go tits up and were left with some different companies. I don't care. But we're left with a whole lot of infrastructure in terms of compute and tooling at data centers, right? In terms of open models and software and hardware that goes together. And really importantly, we'll be left with a lot of institutional learning, right? We're going through this, we're learning to use these tools whether we like it or not. We, if we are smart, can use this as an opportunity to decide which aspects we want to use and where, right? Using consumer, and this is a key point I want to make, using consumer grade tools in an educational research setting is stupid because they're not designed for that, right? They're designed for chatting about, okay, what should I do? I've got three hours in Middlesbrough, what would be the main sites to see, okay?

slides.heading: The upstream build-out is vast — and highly concentrated
slides.body: The boom is leaving behind real infrastructure, but that infrastructure is being financed and controlled by a remarkably small set of actors.

stats.label: top three cloud providers' share of the public-cloud market in 2025
Alphabet's signalled 2026 capital expenditure, largely for technical infrastructure
strategic AI partnerships and GPU commitments shaping the frontier stack

notes.transition: Now show the scale and concentration numerically.
points: Use these figures to make the upstream stack feel vast and unevenly owned.
The point is not memorising exact numbers; it is seeing how far beyond single-university scale this market operates.
This helps explain why universities are mostly downstream actors unless they collaborate strategically.

voiceover.transcript: And I don't want to dwell on this, but the actual money being spent and the scale of this build out and the boom is absolutely spectacular. So it's quick examples, $185 billion is what Alphabet said they were going to spend in capital expenditure. Like data centers, basically, that's just one of the main players, two thirds of all the public cloud market is shared by the top three cloud providers. Hi AWS, sponsor of this conference. And there are basically trillions of dollars in strategic partnerships of GPU commitments back and forth. Now, a lot of this money is entirely circular, which means that when the buck stops, companies will collapse. But again, I don't care. The stock market will care and probably care a little bit, but fundamentally, what's left over will be useful. These data centers will still be useful.

slides.heading: But infrastructure built in a frenzy can still produce dependency
slides.body: Build-outs do not guarantee healthy outcomes. The same boom that creates useful capacity can also create habits of lock-in, misallocated effort and narrow models of progress.

notes.transition: Then add the caution.
points: A boom can create dependency as easily as it creates capability.
Emphasise generosity, convenience, and rapid integration as the conditions under which lock-in often begins.
This is the place to say that infrastructure built in a frenzy often embeds someone else's priorities.
Lock-in during generosity: Deep discounts and rapid integrations make dependence feel painless while switching costs are low and still rising.
Misplaced priorities: Leaders can chase visible partnerships and shiny features while neglecting pedagogy, people and core digital resilience.
Capture of norms: The first systems to scale often end up defining what counts as modern, efficient or inevitable.

voiceover.transcript: So that's a really positive aspect of the bubble, but what we also have with the bubble because there's so much money flying in people, these companies, are selling these things below cost and that's really important to know, right? There's a lot of leaders, right? And they're being very generous, they're giving away, burning all this energy, burning money basically, right, to try and get people locked into their ecosystem. And this means there's lots of opportunities for misplaced priorities. They're trying to please everyone, they're trying to get everyone locked in, they will promise the world, but things are not designed for specific use cases. And in our world, the HE sector seems like everything, but it's a small fry compared to larger business world and the larger consumer world. So we'll get kind of left out. And we don't want either a business model, and we don't really want a consumer model. We want our own higher education specific approaches. The priorities are not the same. But what happens is, as these huge companies are spending all this money, the norms are set, right? What expectations are what is possible and what's not. And coming from a technical background, that's a real shame, because these models offer tremendous opportunity if we want to design and use them ourselves, right, to do what we want to do. But if it's just going to be a consumer chatbot or enterprise chatbot, that's not any use to us.

notes.transition: Now move from capacity to power.
points: This section asks who actually sets the defaults embedded in the stack.
Use the subtitle to signal that the issue is not only lock-in but agenda-setting and mission drift.
We are moving from 'how much is being built?' to 'whose assumptions shape what gets built?'

voiceover.transcript: So that leads me on to who gets to set these defaults, who decides it, and that is people, that is users, right? We have the opportunity to help set the defaults and I'll talk about who is currently setting them and hopefully how we can start to set them.

slides.heading: A small number of firms increasingly control every layer
slides.body: AI is not arriving in higher education as a neutral marketplace of interchangeable tools. A concentrated set of firms controls key model, cloud and interface layers, leaving universities mostly downstream users of systems shaped elsewhere.

notes.transition: Start with the stack itself.
points: Explain that universities are not buying from a flat market of interchangeable tools.
Show the layered dependence: model providers, cloud providers, and interface/platform providers.
Use the scale asymmetry to explain why universities are usually downstream even when they are important customers.
Model layer: A handful of providers dominate frontier model access and shape the pace of release.
Infrastructure and interface: Cloud concentration and campus platforms together shape cost, continuity, distribution and defaults.
Scale asymmetry: Universities will rarely win by outspending the frontier, so leverage has to come from strategy, collaboration and governance.

voiceover.transcript: There's a very small number of firms that are controlling this AI stack, the hardware, the software, the inference, all these sort of things. These firms control everything, and we are purely downstream users. We are not going to be competing with them on scale. That's kind of why I went through all that investment. We do not have 185 billion pounds a year to invest in hardware, and nor should we. Our aim should be to use the hardware that will be left in the wayside, okay, one or two generations old, that'll be perfectly good for what we want to do, and use that. We shouldn't be trying to compete with them, right? We're not starting our own AI frontier lab. We're thinking about, okay, how can these tools, these approaches, all this hardware that's been built out, be used for our own ends?

slides.heading: The real question is whose goals the technology serves
slides.body: Technology does not choose its own social purpose. In universities, the central question is whose goals are being encoded when AI is sold as productivity, innovation or student experience.

notes.transition: Then make the normative move.
points: Technology does not arrive with its social purpose already settled.
Stress that who decides the purpose of a system matters as much as what the system can do.
Bring the argument fully into higher education by asking whose goals are being encoded.
Direction matters: AI can be used to remove drudgery and expand human capability, or to centralise control and reduce labour.
Competing incentives: Management, vendor and platform incentives overlap imperfectly with educational purpose.
Academic and student interests: Universities are supposed to privilege learning, judgment, accessibility, care and public value.

voiceover.transcript: That's where we come back again to the incentives and the direction. So what these tools are for is super important. A general purpose tool doesn't exist. These tools are designed for certain purposes. They are good at lots of different things. I can give an exam paper to a consumer model, and it will do a good job. That's not the point, because we're not looking for answers. We're looking for, OK, how well learning is being grappled with those ILOs in that exam, for instance. It's a totally different type of question. And it might be subtle to those outside. But in HE, it's super, super important. And the incentives are not aligned. There are incentives by these companies to sell and to get people using these tools, especially university. It's why historically big companies like Adobe, et cetera, always gave free licenses to universities to get students using these tools and use them in their work life later on. And we don't want that to happen here. And also, in terms of academic and student interests, it doesn't map in that we want very different things. We're not looking for the end result. We're looking for something to help us with the process. And that is a fundamentally different approach that isn't tackled with these consumer models.

slides.heading: Procurement teams set the agenda
slides.body: The deepest risk is not only lock-in but agenda-setting. Mission drift enters through reasonable purchases that gradually rewrite what counts as normal teaching infrastructure, research workflow and institutional analytics.

notes.transition: Deepen the risk from lock-in to agenda-setting.
points: Mission drift usually arrives through sensible local decisions, not dramatic declarations.
Bundled convenience, default pathways, and contract terms rewrite the environment gradually.
This is where the talk becomes clearly about power, not just procurement.
Bundled convenience: AI arrives inside familiar productivity suites and platforms, making deeper dependency feel incremental.
Default pathways: Institution-wide licences quietly define which tools staff and students see as normal or approved.
Contractual gravity: Once workflows, content and reporting are built into one stack, future decisions become less free than they appear.

voiceover.transcript: So we have to try and set the agenda, but the agenda is normally actually set via procurement. So we have to be really careful and really clear with our procurement teams, I'm sure some procurement colleagues here, about what is needed, what's required, what we're buying, because what we procure becomes the default. It becomes the whole envelope of possibilities. Because once we're in there, the defaults are set, these contracts are long, they're expensive, they'll probably be renewed because, okay, we can't move off. We have to think about procurement and not lock ourselves in to one way of thinking. We have to really try and be sensible of what we're trying to do as a university and why we're trying to do it.

slides.text: If universities buy into a stack on vendor terms, the infrastructure is decided before it's debated.
slides.highlight: infrastructure is decided before it's debated

notes.transition: Let the central warning crystallise.
points: This is one of the strongest lines in the deck, so give it air.
The point is that infrastructure settles before institutions have fully debated its consequences.
Use it to introduce a procurement implication: the way universities buy now will shape the future they are still able to contest.

voiceover.transcript: So just to rest on that point, we have to decide the terms, not the vendors. So procurement, they become our absolute heroes, our champions. They have to push for what we want as universities, rather than what is being offered, right? And if what is being offered doesn't match it, we say, no, thank you. Right? We can go a different path, right? Which we'll talk about at the end of this talk.

notes.transition: After the procurement implication, move back into what dependence actually does inside the university.
points: This section asks what dependence changes in pedagogy, data and educational purpose.
Signal that the next slides are about educational consequences, not just market structure.
This is where the three opening lenses start paying off most clearly.

voiceover.transcript: Okay, so in the next section, I'd like to talk a little bit about more what this dark path, what this look and dependence, what we sleepwalk into living on vendor terms, what does that look like?

slides.heading: The crucial divide is between augmentation and substitution
slides.body: The most important design decision is not whether AI is present. It is whether AI is used to amplify human expertise or to thin it out.

notes.transition: Open with the central distinction.
points: The most important divide is augmentation versus substitution.
Say that the question is not whether AI is present, but what kind of relationship to human expertise the institution is normalising.
This becomes the test you keep applying to the rest of the examples.
Augmentation: AI can remove admin burden, scaffold formative feedback and support accessibility so staff can focus on judgment and contact.
Substitution: AI can also be used to replace teaching presence, automate first-pass support and standardise assessment in thinner ways.
Institutional test: Every use case should be judged by whether it improves educational experience and professional autonomy, not merely cost.

voiceover.transcript: First, I'll have to clarify different uses and types of AI use. First of all, there's augmentation, having these models and systems help us do what we want to do, enable us to do our more human things. And there are lots and lots of possibilities. And I'm not at all anti-AI, I'm very pro-AI. But I think it's got to be used in the very right way. What's more troublesome is pure substitution, when whole aspects of our educational system and our research system are being replaced, end-to-end, black box, that's really, really quite worrying. Because what we are not doing is this institutional test of, is this what we need to do as our institution? Not in terms of efficiency, in terms of jobs to be done, but in terms of our philosophy. Is this what we need? We will have to change. But are we going to change, in a way, the maps to our principles? Because I think anyone who wants to keep a university from 10, 20 years ago, it's not going to happen. Universities will change. But we can still change while still keeping our principles there, because we adapt to these new tools.

slides.heading: The easiest uses to scale are often the least educationally valuable
slides.body: Universities under pressure are especially vulnerable to AI uses that look efficient because they industrialise surface tasks. The more defensible uses usually complement human work instead of trying to replace it.

notes.transition: Now name the tempting failure mode.
points: The most tempting uses are attractive because they scale quickly under financial and workload pressure.
But the better uses are usually the harder ones: admin relief, accessibility and formative scaffolding that preserve human judgment.
Say this is where distortion shows up most clearly.
Substitution at scale: Industrialised content, automated feedback and surveillance systems can look efficient while thinning educational value.
Augmentation where it counts: Admin relief, accessibility support and formative scaffolds are often more defensible because they complement human work.
Institutional test: Ask whether the use deepens educational experience and professional autonomy, not merely whether it is cheap to scale.

voiceover.transcript: It's especially dangerous when we start to push out the substitution aspect at scale, right? Especially if there's cost savings involved. Again, finances are pretty dire across the sector. In many cases, there'll be a desire for, okay, where can we save money? And that is a really dangerous road. You have to be very careful with what we replace with AI. Because what we can do is augment and make better what we do, right? That is definitely a possibility. But again, we have to meet the institutional test of, does this match with our institutional principles?

slides.heading: Pedagogy starts to bend around what the platform can measure
slides.body: Once AI tools are tightly integrated, course design can start adapting to the system rather than the other way round. That is how commercial logic becomes pedagogic logic.

notes.transition: Bring the distortion lens directly into pedagogy.
points: Platforms nudge teaching toward what they can count, standardise, and render visible.
Say that over time pedagogy can start adapting to platform logic rather than educational purpose.
This is a subtle but central claim: metrics and affordances become curricular pressure.
Standardisation pressure: Assessments and activities may drift toward formats that are easy to auto-score, summarise or monitor.
Surface metrics: Completion, engagement and usage are privileged because they are measurable, even when they are weak proxies for learning.
Platform visibility: Platforms and publishers can become the default route through which content, prompts and resources are surfaced.

voiceover.transcript: And as an example, again, for Mernhart, as a teaching and learning-focused academic, when we have set tools, the pedagogy starts to bend around those tools and what a platform can do and what a platform can measure. And there's standardization is an inevitable output of that. You can see this when we're using Moodle or Blackboard, you know, there used to be perhaps many more different, varied, weird and wonderful types of assessment, but when it has to be collapses, now there are different benefits to a VLE versus pen and paper and where before, but there is that standardization pressure and using these tools will only allow certain things. And so those are the things that will get done. Also, we're coming at this from getting these tools from the Silicon Valley, a big tech company where they want to measure anything. Analytics is a real selling point of these things, but these are surface metrics. OK, usage, who logged on and how long were they on for? Not what they were doing, what they learned, was there sort of any aha moments, all these things, right?

slides.heading: Priceless educational data will be captured unless it is governed
slides.body: Universities sit on rich corpora of writing, interaction and workflow data. Once AI layers are integrated, those traces become commercially valuable far beyond their immediate educational use unless institutions treat them as governed assets.

notes.transition: Now bring in extraction.
points: Educational data does not only support services; it can be pulled into upstream value capture.
Explain behavioural exhaust, model improvement, upstream value capture and governance in plain institutional terms.
The room should hear that data governance is about political economy, not only compliance.
Behavioural exhaust: Prompts, drafts, clicks, revisions and case interactions all become telemetry.
Upstream capture: Value created from public educational activity can be captured upstream and turned into model advantage elsewhere.
Governed asset: Separate operational delivery, evaluation and model-improvement uses rather than letting them blur together.

voiceover.transcript: And this last one is really, really important. So we are sitting on a goldmine of educational data. And this is what is termed by the industry as behavioral exhaust, which is both bafflingly dystopian and also vaguely funny. So it's behavioral exhaust as in everything we do is being recorded and everything done in AI is being recorded. And this is of amazing value. And I think this is something where if you take anything away from today, think about every single bit of work you've done at a university, every single bit of work a student's done at university, that whole journey, all the drafts, all the submissions, all the feedback, that is incredibly rich data with which to help build AI systems that continue that system, right? But instead of utilizing that data, we are freely giving it to large tech companies for no apparent reason, right? And they are using that data as value is being captured upstream. We are paying for the privilege handsomely when we could in fact be using that money to help in certain cases build our own models to do our own thing. Okay, if you want to summarize an email, use a commercial model, right? But if you want to really understand learning gain, right, for instance, or anything from pedagogy, we need to use all this data we have, the expertise we have already. And this data, this behavioral exhaust, this educational data, which we are sitting on a goldmine of should be a governed, strongly protected asset of our universities.

notes.transition: After the data implication, move from effects to process.
points: This section explains how convenience hardens into dependence over time.
Say that platforms rarely begin with a visible squeeze; they usually begin with generosity.
The audience should recognise a lifecycle, not a conspiracy.

voiceover.transcript: Now, in the next section, I'll talk about, okay, why am I so wary of these big companies? And that's because we look to history, right? These tech companies, there's a playbook, and it's not necessarily through bias or sometimes it's through malice. It's more through a nature of VC-backed tech companies.

slides.heading: Convenience becomes dependence in stages
slides.body: Platform capture rarely begins with a visible squeeze. It begins with generous access and genuine usefulness, then shifts toward buyer logic, monetisation and harder exit once the institution has adapted.

notes.transition: Start the lifecycle as a sequence rather than a series of separate beats.
points: Early-stage platforms often feel like public goods: low prices, credits, rapid integration and real usefulness.
Over time optimisation shifts toward telemetry, enterprise buyers and monetisation.
By the time the squeeze is felt, the institution has often already adapted around the platform.
Generosity: Subsidised access, rapid value and soft terms make experimentation feel costless.
Squeeze: Pricing, feature access and strategic direction change once dependence is deeper.
Lock-in: Workflows, habits and data structures make leaving much harder than it looked at the start.

voiceover.transcript: The first part is that they have a lot of money, right? They have a lot of capital, venture capital, and they have to expand and get big quickly. So they're very generous, right? Be this with free version of chat GPT, which is basically burning money. This is Uber, right? When they move to a city, they're very cheap. And then once they have basically taken over, the price is all ramping up. Because then you've got the squeeze, okay, the rise of prices, reduce the options, things become paid add-ons, right? They've got you for longer and longer contracts, and you're basically locked in. And this is the standard playbook. And again, not necessarily done intentionally, although frequently is, but it's just a nature of their business. They have all this money, and sometime, at some point, they've got to start turning those handles, turning that ratchet to get out some profit, and it inevitably happens.

slides.heading: Path dependence becomes governance
slides.body: Early choices about tools, contracts and norms do not stay local or temporary. They accumulate into architecture. Standards harden, investments compound and alternatives narrow, so procurement quietly becomes governance.

notes.transition: Name the mechanism.
points: This is path dependence at institutional scale.
Today's local decisions accumulate into tomorrow's constrained options.
Say plainly that procurement becomes governance because procurement shapes architecture.
Standards harden: File formats, APIs, assessment rules and reporting practices settle around early winners.
Investment compounds: Training, integrations and custom workflows make the original choice seem ever more rational.
Options narrow: Code, market structure and institutional norms together shape what later choices still feel realistic.

voiceover.transcript: And this is insidious because that generosity might seem great initially, but it means standards are set early, right? Any investment on their part and in our part compounds and we get more and more locked in and our options narrow, right? You get less and less flexibility. The more we buy into Anthropic or OpenAI or Microsoft Copilot, whatever it might be, okay, our options get narrower and narrower because we're just building, we're investing more, more knowledge, more, more effort on this one tool. Why spread ourselves thinly when we want to concentrate on one thing? I make the argument we should be spreading ourselves more thinly.

slides.heading: Prefer modular architecture and plural ecosystems
slides.body: The goal is not to build everything locally. It is to avoid allowing one stack to become the only imaginable path.

notes.transition: Turn the right of exit into an architectural principle.
points: Do not let one stack become the only imaginable route through the institution.
Modularity and plural ecosystems preserve bargaining power, resilience and room to adapt.
This is how you keep options open before convenience hardens into culture.

voiceover.transcript: So what should we do instead? Think about modular design. We should be using all the providers. I use a ton of AI in my spin-out company, from coding, especially to designing models or to data science, and I use all the main providers. I have accounts with all of them. They're all good at different things. Different models are good at different things. I have some magentic workflows that use open AI and anthropic models and open source models all at the same time for very different things for different speeds, right? We should have that exposure, be able to pick the best thing, right? Have the right size model. We shouldn't be using just what's given to us, be it some frankly crappy co-pilot model or some huge frontier model that's very wasteful. We should decide what's right for us, and we should experiment and have the ability to plug and play with the different models that are given by providers or as time goes on and the open source models become much more prevalent. Perhaps those open source models are even better, are fine-tuned versions of those models, fine-tuned on all that really rich educational data that we've got.

slides.title: A different strategy
slides.subtitle: Engaged stewardship instead of passive consumption

notes.transition: Now gather the earlier implications into a more coherent strategy.
points: The audience has already seen governance, procurement, data and architecture implications earlier in the talk.
This section consolidates those scattered implications into an explicit stewardship posture.
The answer remains neither refusal nor surrender; it is now being named more fully.

voiceover.transcript: So Stuart, you've talked about how bad these companies are, you talked about how much data we have, the possibilities and resources we have, the expertise we have, what we should be doing, right? How can we actually do this? What would be the

slides.heading: The strongest response is neither refusal nor surrender
slides.body: Universities do not need a fantasy of total sovereignty, and they should not accept passive dependence either. The workable position is engaged stewardship.

notes.transition: State the third position clearly.
points: The strongest response is engaged stewardship.
Say that universities may buy some things, build or host some things, adapt some things, and refuse some things.
The important point is doing that from deliberate governance rather than drift.
Buy some things: Use commercial infrastructure where it is genuinely useful and where the terms are governable.
Build or host selectively: Keep local or sector-controlled options for high-value or high-risk uses where continuity, privacy or contestability matter most.
The point is not to recreate frontier labs; it is to hold enough capability to negotiate, evaluate and keep critical educational functions under institutional control.
Refuse some things: Reject deployments that undermine educational purpose even if they look efficient on paper.

voiceover.transcript: So, first of all, I would say a strong response is neither total refusal, I don't really have much time for people to say no AI at all, I don't think that's sensible or helpful. I also don't think we should surrender to a big company, get all our branded t-shirts on and just go, kumbaya, let's go open AI on Thropic, something in between. So we want to buy some things, right? These tools are extremely useful, useful tools are worth money, we should buy some, right? But we should be very selective with what we buy, not lock ourselves in and not let them dictate how we use it and what it's used for. We find a use case, we get the right tool for the job. We should also build or host our own AI, right, selectively. This is a big one, this is something no one's doing. As a sector, we have the expertise, we have the data, especially collectively, if I've learned anything from my work in student voice, is that these models get so much better when there is multiple institutions involved. It's a much richer data set, a much wider distribution of data. So we can build and host selectively. And we can also, for those people who want to refuse things, refuse some things. We don't have to use it for everything. We can just say, no, we're keeping that analog, human, for these reasons, based on our principles as an institution. I think that is entirely defensible.

slides.heading: Start from public-interest goals, not adoption goals
slides.body: An AI strategy should define what the university is trying to protect and improve before it defines what tools it plans to deploy.

notes.transition: Start the constructive section from purpose.
points: Strategy has to begin with what the university is trying to protect and improve, not with tool adoption targets.
Use human contact, inclusion, and educational purpose as guardrails.
This keeps the policy agenda tied to educational purpose rather than IT modernisation alone.
Human contact: Use AI to create more time for mentoring, feedback, discussion and care.
Inclusion and accessibility: Prioritise translation, captioning, alternative formats and scaffolded support.
Educational purpose: Explicitly reject headcount reduction or content automation in place of teaching as primary aims.

voiceover.transcript: So what might some of those institutional goals be, or principles? One is that human contact is important, right? Okay, we're not bringing ourselves down to purely, okay, this personnel knows more stuff, kicks them out the door. It's about building human relationships, trust, right? Human contact is important, right? And AI can help with that, but it has to be very on the edges, right? We want to maximize human contact. We want to have a goal of maximizing inclusion and accessibility. These tools can really help with that, right? There are lots of people who find information far more accessible, right, through these AI tools, and they can be more inclusive, right, if used correctly, if they are matched to the educational purpose, right? That's the ultimate principle, especially on the educational side, right? That we have these goals, we have to meet those rather than saying, okay, we're only using AI by whatever date. Instead, it's like, how can we push forward our goals?

slides.text: Make evaluation a core institutional capability

notes.transition: Then move to evaluation.
points: Evaluation is the quiet centrepiece of stewardship.
Say that universities cannot rely on demos, generic benchmarks, or marketing claims for high-stakes uses.
Stress task-based testing, independent scrutiny, and continuous review.
Task-based testing: Build benchmark tasks for feedback, retrieval, advising, accessibility and disciplinary use cases.
Independent scrutiny: Review hallucination risk, bias, privacy leakage, citation behaviour and fail-safe performance.
Continuous review: Re-evaluate when underlying models, terms or integrations change upstream.

voiceover.transcript: So key to any strategy, right? Key to what I've said before, buy some things, build some things and refuse some things, is make evaluation a core institutional capability. We will not know what to buy, what to build, what to refuse, if we don't have any way to measure the efficacy of these models. So evaluation is key. There's something I've learned building these models for decades, is that it's all about evaluation, all about the test dataset, all about the gold standard dataset you've hand labeled, that is what you need. You need to be able to evaluate these things. And evaluation can happen individually, but it can also happen collectively.

slides.heading: Sector collaboration and public alternatives matter
slides.body: No single university has enough leverage to negotiate every clause, evaluate every model or build every critical service alone. Collaboration is a practical necessity, including where the sector decides to build some capacity for itself.

notes.transition: Finish the section by scaling outward.
points: Single universities are too small to negotiate, evaluate, and build everything alone.
Sector collaboration and public alternatives are not idealism; they are a realistic response to asymmetric markets.
This lets you sound practical without sounding institutionally isolated.
Shared procurement: Use sector bodies and consortia to negotiate stronger data terms, exit rights and auditability.
Shared evaluation: Build common test suites, model cards and red-teaming practices for educational use cases.

Public-interest infrastructure: Explore national, regional or mission-based alternatives where the market produces choke points, including shared hosting or model-building where that is strategically sensible.

voiceover.transcript: So that collectivism, I think, is important. We can share things, right? We can share procurement. Why don't, in terms of, okay, we get a joint contract to use a certain provider, right? But also perhaps more importantly, we could build some of these tools ourselves, right? We have all the expertise needed, right? And we're not just talking, okay, the computer science department, right? We're talking education department, the social sciences department, the admin experience, the transformation teams, procurement, everything, right? We have all of the people, every university, and joined together, we could build something spectacular if we put our minds to it, right? There's nothing stopping us, right? The stage is set. There'll be hardware all there. The boom will come. The bubble will burst. The hardware will be left. We're in a great position with these fantastic open source models, all this available hardware, experience using these tools, massive evaluation data set, right? To build these valuations, to say, okay, this is what we want to do. This is how we can know if it's good or not, right?

slides.heading: Ownership means owning some of the stack
slides.body: Contracts matter, but universities do not really shape their AI future if every consequential layer remains external. Ownership in practice means deciding which parts of compute, models and oversight must stay institutionally or sector governed.

notes.transition: Make ownership concrete rather than abstract.
points: Say that ownership is not only about procurement clauses; it is also about who controls key layers of the system.
The claim is not that every university should own the entire stack.
The claim is that some compute, some models and some evaluation capacity should be institutionally or sector governed where mission, resilience or contestability matter.
Compute where it matters: Retain local or shared hosting for high-value functions where continuity, privacy or bargaining power are strategically important.
Models for university tasks: Build, adapt or fine-tune models for bounded educational and administrative use cases rather than relying only on general-purpose systems.
Evaluation under our control: Keep benchmark design, red-teaming and monitoring in hands that are accountable to educational values.

voiceover.transcript: And that means taking some ownership of this, right? And that might be ownership of different aspects, layers of the stack. Okay, in terms of compute, that might not be owning our own data center, but it'll be having contracts with people like AWS to help power that, right? It's absolutely great. We'll be looking to own some models, right? Collectively, I think would be the best way to do this. And again, this is ambitious, right? But people said that about student voice AI as well, that universities would never share their data and multiple universities have for the better good. I think this could work, right? And importantly, that big evaluation data set is under our control. We decide if it's doing the thing we want it to do or not, right? We get to decide if this works, so yay or nay. Not someone else, not some product manager who has very different incentives to us, right? We get to decide.

slides.heading: The AI boom creates a window for shared sector capacity
slides.body: As hardware markets soften and open-weight models mature, the opportunity is to build common services around university-specific tasks rather than repeat the frontier race at smaller scale.

notes.transition: Then show why this is newly plausible.
points: Make this the practical follow-on from ownership: shared capacity is how the sector avoids every institution solving the same problems alone.
Stress the modesty of the claim: not frontier-model rivalry, but reliable shared services for bounded university tasks.
Shared compute pools: As hardware depreciates and supply loosens, some compute may become affordable for shared sector hosting or bounded institutional workloads.
University-specific services: Open-weight and smaller models are increasingly good enough for retrieval, classification, transcription, analysis and support tasks common in universities.
Common assurance: Education and social science are needed alongside computer science to define tasks, evaluate harms and shape institutional fit.

voiceover.transcript: And coming off this boom, the bubble bursting, we have a real opportunity for shared compute, for university-specific services, and common assurance.

slides.text: The alternative to passive dependence is not heroic self-sufficiency. It is practical stewardship.

notes.transition: State the positive identity of the argument.
points: The alternative to passive dependence is practical stewardship.
This line matters because it keeps the keynote from sounding like a sovereignty fantasy.
Pause slightly; this is the constructive counterpart to the earlier warnings.

voiceover.transcript: But what I'm not saying is here, is that we should build everything ourself. This is, again, a common trap in higher education, is that, okay, we'll build our own thing, and it'll be really tight and just kept up. No. The alternative to sort of becoming entirely passive independent on these big companies is not doing everything ourselves. It's practical stewardship, using the hardware that will be available, using the open source tools that will be available, working together to build something where it's not being supplied by these vendors. Again, if you want to summarize an email, there's simple models for that, but there's more complex things around research, administration, especially education, right? We want to be in control.

slides.title: What the next decade looks like
slides.subtitle: Two futures are still possible

notes.transition: Move from strategy to consequences.
points: This final section asks what the next decade looks like under two different patterns of choice.
Use the subtitle to frame the end as a genuine fork, not a foregone conclusion.
The tone here should become slightly more civic and decision-oriented.

voiceover.transcript: So in this last section, I'd like to give you some scenarios of what a good outcome might look like and what a less good outcome might look like.

slides.section: What the next decade looks like
slides.heading: If universities play this moment well
slides.body: The optimistic future is not one in which universities beat hyperscalers at their own game. It is one in which they use the boom to build durable, plural and publicly oriented capability.

notes.transition: Paint the better future concretely.
points: A good future is not universities beating hyperscalers at their own game.
It is cheaper infrastructure on better terms, human-centred pedagogy, and stronger public capability.
Keep the picture specific enough to feel plausible rather than utopian.
Cheaper infrastructure, better terms: Institutions inherit mature tools and compute without surrendering all bargaining power.
Human-centred pedagogy: AI is used to deepen feedback, accessibility and high-value contact rather than thin them out.
Stronger public capacity: Sector collaboration creates standards, evaluation routines and alternatives that keep the system contestable.

voiceover.transcript: What makes a good outcome look like? So, post-boom, we have cheaper infrastructure, better terms, more flexibility, right? We have the ability to have all these models help with human-centered pedagogy, right? Rather than shoving things down people's throats on these consumer models, instead, we design models based on educational research and best practices to help people learn, right? We also have the opportunity, as universities, because we have all of these strengths, we have everything in place. Like I say, we'll have access to the post-boom hardware, we'll have access to open-source models. We've got all the expertise, of course, computer science, social science, admin, transformation, right? We've got this huge amount of data, this evaluation data set we can use. That's everything you need to create a literally world-class model. We could show the way for public sector bodies, groups, right, using these tools for our own principles, for our own direction.

slides.section: What the next decade looks like
slides.heading: If universities drift into platform dependency
slides.body: The pessimistic future is not spectacular collapse. It is a quieter form of dependency in which institutions keep functioning while their freedom, pluralism and educational quality erode.

notes.transition: Then contrast it with the quieter bad future.
points: The dependency future is not dramatic collapse; it is ongoing functioning under worsening external control.
Emphasise rent extraction, thinner provision, and opaque governance.
The point is to show what slow loss of autonomy looks like in ordinary institutional life.
Rent extraction: AI becomes a permanent premium layer across teaching, admin, assessment and research workflows.
Thin provision: Lower-status students and institutions get automated, standardised provision while wealthier parts of the sector preserve human-rich models.
Opaque governance: Research, pedagogy and student support are increasingly shaped by systems the university cannot fully audit or challenge.

voiceover.transcript: But what else could happen? If we just buy into these consumer or enterprise contracts, these models designed for businesses or consumers are not designed for our use cases in universities, then we get locked in. We are basically paying rent to use these things. We're giving away all of our data. The options get less and less, and we have none of that transparency, none of that visibility into what is being created, what is being delivered, how these models are changing over time. Basically, a very bad situation, and that's equally possible.

slides.section: What the next decade looks like
slides.heading: The next few years require three decisions from leaders
slides.body: Every institution does not need the same answer, but all of them need to decide what they will refuse to outsource by default.

points.title: What must we retain control over?
What should we build or evaluate together?

notes.transition: Convert the contrast into leadership choices.
points: Every institution has to decide what it will refuse to outsource by default.
Use the three questions as decision tests: what must we retain control over, what should we build or evaluate together, and what rights should students have?
This sets up the closing agenda cleanly.
What must we retain control over?: Define the teaching, assessment, data or support functions where exit, auditability and human oversight are non-negotiable.
What should we build or evaluate together?: Identify the areas where sector collaboration creates more leverage than acting alone.
What rights should students have?: Make explicit the rights to transparency, contestability, accessibility and non-AI pathways in core processes.

voiceover.transcript: So fundamentally, the next few years require three big decisions. Is that what do we want to retain control over? What aspects of AI are we wanting to control? Because I believe there are aspects we should be controlling. Again, we buy some things, but we build and control some things too. Also, of course, for the refusedics, also refuse some things. There's no problem about saying, this thing is done old school. No problem at all. It's when you say, I'm not using any AI or any computers at all in my life, you become slightly extreme, and you're really cutting off your nose to spite your face. So what should we build or evaluate together? And importantly, what rights should students have? What rights should the people involved in this process? They don't want to be taught by an AI bot. If they did, they could go in and do that. It comes down to being lecture recordings. It's like, you could just be taught by on YouTube. Yeah, you could. You could absolutely get a world-class education watching YouTube. You could learn as an aerospace engineer, a PhD. You could get an M.Eng. aerospace just by watching YouTube and just taking some exams. But people don't want to do that. That's not how most people learn. If you can learn that way, if you're an autodidact, go for it. That's not how students want to learn. So how do students want to learn? What rights should they have around their data, what models they're exposed to? That's really important.

slides.section: What the next decade looks like
slides.text: Universities are not only deciding whether to use AI. They are helping fix the defaults of higher education for years.

notes.transition: Lift back from the checklist to the stakes.
points: The point is bigger than whether to deploy a particular tool.
Universities are helping set the defaults that will structure higher education for years.
This is the right slide to widen the frame before the final question.

voiceover.transcript: So what might the next decade look like? Well, we as universities aren't deciding, okay, should we use AI or not, right? We're deciding what are the defaults, right, for the next decades, okay? What is education going to look like, right? Either we get to decide, or someone's going to decide for us.

slides.text: If AI becomes part of higher education's infrastructure, who should control it, and what kind of university should it serve?

notes.transition: End with the question rather than another answer.
points: Let the closing question hang for a moment and hand neatly into discussion or panel.
The point is to force the room to connect infrastructure control with the kind of university it wants AI to serve.
Do not rush the last line; it is the civic and institutional landing point.

voiceover.transcript: And a final closing question for you all, something to go away and think about, if AI becomes part of higher education infrastructure, who should control it? And what kind of universities should it serve? Universities will change, right? But I believe the best change will be when we control the AI and use AI to make the change ourselves, retain that autonomy. Thank you very much.
